topic: rainy season prep — covering cart and protecting charcoal

turns.content: RAIN na ka. so much rain. I am looking at the weather forecast for next week and it is rain every single day 😭 I need help thinking about how to protect my setup
Rainy season is no joke when you're working outdoors! What's your current setup like for rain protection? Do you have any covering over your cart right now?

I have a basic tarp that I put up on poles na ka. like a roof over the cart. but when the wind blow, the rain come sideways and everything get wet. and the BIGGEST problem is the charcoal. if charcoal get wet it is FINISHED. cannot light, cannot use. I lost a whole bag last week because I didn't cover it in time 😩

Losing a whole bag of charcoal to rain is painful — both the cost and the fact that you can't just dry it out and use it. Wet charcoal is basically ruined. The sideways rain issue is tricky with a tarp alone.

A few ideas for protecting the charcoal specifically:
1. **Waterproof storage bin** — keep all your charcoal in a sealed plastic bin with a lid. Only take out what you need.
2. **Plastic sheeting on the sides** — hang clear plastic sheets from your tarp poles on the windward sides. Cheap and you can roll them up when it's not raining.
3. **Elevated storage** — keep the charcoal bags off the ground on a small platform or milk crate so ground water doesn't creep up.

For the sideways rain hitting your cooking area, those side curtains would be the biggest improvement. What direction does the rain usually come from?

usually from the east na ka!! so I can put the plastic sheet on the east side and maybe south side too. the tarp is about 3 meter by 3 meter which cover the cart but not much space around it. the waterproof bin for charcoal is SMART. I don't know why I never think of that. I just leave the bag sitting there like an idiot haha

Don't be hard on yourself — when you're busy cooking 350 plates, thinking about charcoal storage isn't exactly top of mind! A big plastic bin with a snap-on lid would solve the problem completely. You can get those at any Tesco Lotus or HomePro for like 200-300 baht. Get one that fits at least a couple bags so you're not constantly refilling.

For the plastic sheeting, you can get clear rolls at a hardware store. Zip-tie them to your tarp poles on the east and south sides. Clear plastic is better than opaque because customers can still see you cooking, which is half the appeal of street food.

What about your customers? Do they just stand in the rain while waiting?

haha yes poor thing!! some bring umbrella, some just run to the cart and grab the food and run back. during heavy rain my customer number drop a lot. maybe I only sell 200-250 plate instead of 350 on a rainy day. that is 5,000-7,000 baht less revenue na ka 😩 rain is expensive

A 100-plate drop on rainy days is significant — that's basically losing a third of your business to weather. The plastic side curtains might actually help with that too, because if customers see that your cart area is dry and sheltered, they're more likely to stop instead of walking past.

Some cart vendors I've seen set up a small covered waiting area — even just extending the tarp a meter out with a couple extra poles so there's a dry spot for people to stand while they wait. Would your spot allow for that?

hmm maybe na ka. I don't want to block the sidewalk because city hall is strict about that — you know I need my license to be valid and they check these things. but I can maybe extend the tarp a little bit on the customer side. just enough so 3-4 people can stand under it while waiting. that is a good idea!!

Smart to keep it modest — you don't want any issues with the authorities over sidewalk space. Even an extra meter of coverage on the customer side would make a big difference. People are much more willing to wait in a queue when they're not getting soaked.

So your rainy season upgrade plan:
1. Waterproof bin for charcoal storage
2. Clear plastic side curtains on east and south
3. Small tarp extension on customer side

That's probably under 1,000 baht total for materials and could save you thousands in lost revenue on rainy days. When's your next day off to get the supplies?

Sunday!! I can go to HomePro on Sunday na ka. I will get the bin, the plastic roll, some zip tie, and extra tarp. maybe also some sandbag for the pole so the wind don't blow everything away. last month the wind was so strong my tarp almost fly to Chiang Mai haha 😂

Your tarp almost migrating to Chiang Mai is a very real Bangkok rainy season experience! Sandbags on the poles are a great call — much more stable than just pushing the poles into the ground. You could also use those screw-in ground anchors if the surface allows, but sandbags are simpler and you can move them easily.

HomePro on Sunday — sounds like a plan. Get everything set up before next week's rain forecast hits. Your charcoal will thank you, your customers will thank you, and your revenue will thank you.

haha yes!! operation: save the charcoal 😂 okay I will go Sunday morning. Beam can come help me set everything up too. thank you for the ideas na ka I should have done this years ago. every rainy season I just suffer and complain but this year I actually prepare!! goodnight 🙏

Better late than never! This year you go into the rain with a plan instead of just a prayer and a soggy tarp. Beam's extra hands will make the setup quick. Once it's all in place you'll wonder why you didn't do it sooner.

Goodnight! Go conquer HomePro on Sunday and show the rain who's boss. 🙏
